Title: Negative Regulation of Anthocyanin Biosynthesis in Arabidopsis by a miR156-Targeted SPL Transcription Factor

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Branch point downstream step competing with flavonol synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces dihydroflavonols such as dihydrokaempferol to corresponding leucoanthocyanidins, committing flux toward anthocyanin formation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key branch-control enzyme supported by the paper
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Stem base at the junction between rosette and stem; hypocotyl-cotyledon junction; inflorescence internodes
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pigmentation and likely defense-related secondary metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher DFR expression is associated with increased anthocyanin accumulation; repression of DFR is associated with reduced anthocyanin and increased flavonol accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highest in basal stem region and lowest in apical stem region
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Promoter activity strongest at the junction between stem and rosette leaves; enhanced in Pro35S:MIR156 and reduced in Pro35S:MIM156
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Long-day growth; dexamethasone induction of rSPL9-GR with cycloheximide for direct regulation assay
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: qRT-PCR; ProDFR:GUS reporter assay; dexamethasone-inducible rSPL9-GR assay with cycloheximide; ChIP-qPCR
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: LC-MS metabolite profiling of flavonols; no public accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Higher DFR-associated anthocyanin flux is antagonistic to flavonol accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A suitable metabolic engineering node to shift flavonoid flux toward anthocyanins, but changes may reduce flavonol levels
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DFR transcript abundance is strongly induced in Pro35S:MIR156 and reduced in Pro35S:MIM156 plants. 2. DFR expression is inversely correlated with SPL9/SPL15 expression along the stem. 3. DFR promoter activity spatially matches anthocyanin accumulation and is directly associated with SPL9-dependent repression. 4. DFR acts as a major branch-control point determining anthocyanin versus flavonol flux.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although SPL9 is recruited to the DFR promoter, mutation of promoter GTAC motifs did not alter reporter pattern, suggesting indirect recruitment through the MYB-bHLH-WD40 complex.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts dihydroflavonols to flavonols such as kaempferol, competing with DFR for common substrates
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Important competing branch enzyme; not shown here to be transcriptionally controlled by SPL9
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: FLS1 expression was not greatly altered by miR156/SPL manipulation, but flavonol accumulation increased when SPL activity was high because anthocyanin branch genes were repressed
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transcript level not greatly changed in Pro35S:MIM156 or Pro35S:MIR156; not affected by DEX-induced SPL9 activation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: qRT-PCR; DEX-inducible rSPL9-GR expression assay; LC-MS metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: LC-MS metabolite profiling of flavonols; no public accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Enhanced flavonol flux can accompany reduced anthocyanin accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Relevant branch enzyme for redirecting flux toward flavonols versus anthocyanins, likely more effective when combined with regulators such as SPL9
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FLS1 is the major flavonol synthase discussed for Arabidopsis stems. 2. Its transcript level is largely unchanged by altered miR156/SPL activity. 3. The anthocyanin-to-flavonol ratio is primarily shifted through repression of anthocyanin genes rather than direct FLS1 activation in this study.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper emphasizes substrate competition between FLS and DFR as the basis for altered flux.

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: SQUAMOSA PROMOTER BINDING PROTEIN-LIKE (SBP/SPL) transcription factor with SBP DNA-binding domain
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Stem base at rosette-stem junction; hypocotyl-cotyledon junction; inflorescence internodes for anthocyanin; stems for kaempferol derivatives
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pigmentation and secondary metabolism linked to developmental transition and likely defense
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Negative transcriptional regulation via destabilization of an activator complex
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Represses anthocyanin biosynthetic genes including DFR, ANS, F3'H, and UGT75C1; indirectly shifts flux toward flavonols
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Branch selection and metabolic flux allocation in flavonoid biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: DFR; ANS; F3'H; UGT75C1; PAP1-containing MYB-bHLH-WD40 complex
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition- and development-dependent controller that acts as a major negative switch for anthocyanin accumulation during floral transition
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Possible indirect feedback involving MYB12/MYB111 discussed, but not established for SPL9
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Interferes with the PAP1-TT8-TTG1 MYB-bHLH-WD40 activation complex by binding PAP1 and competing with TT8
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: miR156 negatively regulates SPL9; developmental age/floral transition
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Higher in apical stem regions and lower in basal stem regions, opposite to DFR
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Increased SPL activity in Pro35S:MIM156 and rSPL9 overexpression lines correlates with reduced anthocyanin accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Long-day growth; dexamethasone treatment of ProSPL9:rSPL9-GR seedlings
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression analysis; miR156 mimic/overexpression genetics; dexamethasone-inducible GR fusion assay with cycloheximide; ChIP-qPCR; yeast two-hybrid; luciferase complementation/BiFC; coimmunoprecipitation; transient expression competition assay
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: LC-MS metabolite profiling of flavonols; no public accession reported
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Suppressing SPL9 to raise anthocyanins reduces flavonol accumulation and may alter developmental timing because SPL9 also regulates phase change and flowering
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A strong engineering target for increasing anthocyanins or altering anthocyanin/flavonol ratio, but manipulation should consider pleiotropic effects on development and flowering
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: SPL9 decreases anthocyanin accumulation and promotes relative flavonol accumulation by repressing anthocyanin pathway gene expression
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Overexpression of SPL9 decreases anthocyanin accumulation, while reduced SPL activity via miR156 increases anthocyanin levels. 2. SPL9 directly associates with the DFR promoter region and rapidly represses DFR expression after inducible activation. 3. SPL9 binds PAP1/MYB113 and competes with TT8, destabilizing the MYB-bHLH-WD40 complex required for anthocyanin gene activation. 4. SPL9 thereby shifts flavonoid metabolic flux away from anthocyanins toward flavonols.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although SPL9 binds the DFR promoter region, GTAC motif mutation did not change DFR reporter activity, indicating indirect promoter recruitment rather than canonical direct DNA binding at those sites.

Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Reduces SPL9-type SPL activity, thereby indirectly de-repressing anthocyanin biosynthetic genes such as DFR, ANS, F3'H, and UGT75C1
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Flavonoid flux partitioning between anthocyanin and flavonol branches
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: SPL9; SPL10; SPL15 and other miR156-targeted SPL genes
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Upstream developmental regulator that promotes anthocyanin accumulation by repressing negative SPL regulators
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Developmental age; miR156 declines with progression toward flowering as discussed
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: High miR156 is associated with basal stem regions and juvenile state by inference from inverse SPL pattern
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Pro35S:MIR156 increases anthocyanins and lowers flavonols; Pro35S:MIM156 has opposite effects
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic overexpression of MIR156; target mimic MIM156; pigment quantification; LC-MS metabolite profiling; qRT-PCR of pathway genes
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: LC-MS metabolite profiling of flavonols; no public accession reported
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Elevating miR156 changes developmental timing and SPL-controlled traits beyond metabolism
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful upstream lever to enhance anthocyanin accumulation, but broad pleiotropic developmental effects make direct SPL targeting potentially more precise
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increased miR156 promotes anthocyanin accumulation and reduces flavonol accumulation; reduced miR156 activity has opposite effects
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Pro35S:MIR156 plants accumulate about fourfold more stem anthocyanin than wild type. 2. Reduced miR156 activity in Pro35S:MIM156 markedly lowers anthocyanin and increases kaempferol-derivative flavonols. 3. miR156 acts upstream of SPL9-type factors to regulate flavonoid branch flux.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper centers mechanistically on SPL9; miR156 is the upstream regulator establishing SPL dosage.
